heading: libro del trattato dedicato ad Almansore38, e anche Galeno39, Avicenna40, Alberto Magno41, Michele Scoto42, il Conciliatore43 e molti nostri
card_summary: This chunk contains the heart of the Tono Sesto (Sixth Tone) of Zorzi's Cantico Primo, comprising thirty chapters (Caput V–XXXIV) on the human being as microcosm. Zorzi demonstrates that man contains within himself — in hierarchical analogy — all levels of created being: the four elements, stones, metals, mixed minerals, plants, animals, the seven planetary spheres, the sphere of the fixed stars, the angels, and ultimately God himself. His sources range from the Kabbalistic etymology of Adam (alef = divinity, dalet = celestial nature, mem = corruptible matter) and the Hebrew urim ve-tummim, through Hermes Trismegistus, Zoroaster, Pythagoras, Plato, Aristotle, Boethius, and Origen, to Avicenna, Galen, Hippocrates, Albert the Great, Pietro d'Abano, Empedocles, Porphyry, Iamblichus, Proclus, and Augustine. Scriptural anchors include Genesis 2:7 (spiraculum vitarum), Psalms, Leviticus, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Malachias, Apocalypse, and the Gospel of John. The cosmological argument culminates in correspondences between specific human faculties and the seven planets, then between the whole man and the angels, and finally in a treatment of the "membra Dei" — the divine members — as an image of the Trinity in which man participates.
essay:
# Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi* — Tono Sesto, Capp. V–XXXIV: The Human Being as Universal Microcosm

**Note:** This summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (approx. the first 600–800 lines out of ~3,600), supplemented by chapter headings and spot-checks of later chapters. The full section runs from Caput V to Caput XXXIV.

---

## Introduction: Physiognomy and the Context of the Section

The chunk opens mid-argument within a discussion of physiognomy and the reading of character from physical signs — attributed to Pythagoras, to Fraotes king of India (via Apollonius of Tyana), to Julius Caesar and Sulla, and supported by a citation from Job ("Ha inciso sul palmo della mano degli uomini"). Zorzi notes that physicians read bodily constitution from the colour of the face, offering an elaborate Galenic-Avicennian scheme of the four humours and their chromatic signatures. This opening serves as the porch to the main edifice: the systematic demonstration that the whole cosmos is mirrored in man.

---

## Caput V: "Tutto è compreso nell'uomo: discorso generale" (Everything is contained in man: a general discourse)

The central thesis of the entire section is announced: the "sommo Artefice" has constructed man as a recapitulation of all reality. Zorzi's proof-text is Genesis 2:7 — "Formavit Dominus Deus hominem pulverem de terra, et inspiravit in faciem eius spiraculum vitarum" — in which the two extremes of all existence (earth and divine life) are simultaneously named, with everything intermediate implicitly included.

> *"Infimum enim et prae omnibus minoratam est terrae elementum, supremum vero ille, qui ait: Ego sum vita, in omnia videlicet vitae radios diffundens. Et ut omnes vitae gradus in homine conclusos declararet Moses, non ab re dixit spiraculum vitarum, quia omnibus vitae gradibus vivit hic homo."*
<cite>p. 602 (Latin text, Caput V)</cite>

Zorzi then provides a Kabbalistic etymology of the Hebrew name Adam (אדם): alef (א) = divinity; dalet (ד) = celestial nature; mem (מ) = the corporeal and corruptible — thus encoding in man's very name the triad of divine, celestial, and material principles.

Man is said to live simultaneously the life of stones and elements (mere being and alterability), of metals (a hidden vital spirit), of plants (vegetative life), of animals (sensation and motion), of separated intelligences (rational life), and of God himself (divine and eternal life). This graduated vitalism draws on Hermes Trismegistus ("L'uomo è una totalità e un insieme compatto nel tutto"), on Augustine (the soul as "omnium rerum similitudo"), and on Boethius, who describes the harmony of the rational soul in musical terms — grave and acute voices resolved in one consonance.

A Zoroastrian oracle — "L'uomo esulta per l'armonia nella quale il corpo mortale sussiste" — is cited as corroborating testimony. Origen's reading of "Maschio e femmina li creò" is adduced: the male principle signifies the intellect/divine element, the female the animal body, and their harmonious union is the task of human life.

---

## Caput VI: "Con quale eleganza si ritrovano gli elementi nell'uomo"

The formal analogical system begins. The four elements are present in man with greater nobility than in any other composite being, because man alone among living creatures participates in celestial and divine life. Zorzi gives the classical Hippocratic-Galenic ratios of the humours: blood (8), phlegm (4), bile (2), black bile (1) — a series of double proportions which he identifies as constituting a musical harmony. He criticises Pietro d'Abano (the Conciliatore) for following Alexander of Aphrodisias in assigning superpartient ratios to the humours, on the Proclean principle that harmony cannot arise from superpartient proportions.

---

## Caput VII: "Che cosa nell'uomo corrisponde agli elementi"

The five external senses correspond to the four elements: sight to fire; hearing to air; smell and taste to water; touch to earth. This mapping follows Augustine, though Zorzi notes that Francesco di Meyronnes (the "raffinatissimo Francesco") dissents on the assignment of taste to water.

---

## Caput VIII: "A quali pietre corrispondono le parti del corpo"

Stones correspond to bones: both occupy the framework of their respective macrocosm/microcosm. Zorzi cites Empedocles' dictum that "stones are the bones of the world" and the Ovidian verse on Deucalion's stones. He defends a graduated notion of life in stones against Albert the Great's objection (that stones lack porosity for life to penetrate), arguing that life exists at many ontological levels — stones live their one operation (attracting, repelling, gladdening), just as angels, men, plants, and metals each live a different grade. The Psalms (Exultabunt omnia ossa mea; Conturbata sunt omnia ossa mea) are read as evidence that the "bones of the better man" — the spiritual man — also have their counterpart.

---

## Caput IX: "A che cosa corrispondono i metalli"

Bodily humours correspond to metals: both are aqueous in nature, both generated in the "womb" of earth or body under planetary influence. Zorzi synthesises Hermes (Evate, "king of the Arabs"), Ptolemy, Avicenna, Empedocles, Abubacher, and Albert on the generation of metals. Blood is identified as the seat of the soul (Leviticus: "Nel sangue dell'animale risiede la sua anima"), and Hermes Trismegistus is invoked for the doctrine that spirit dwells in blood as its vehicle.

The metals of the interior man then receive an extended allegorical-scriptural treatment: gold = charity (Apocalypse 3:18: "Ti consiglio di comprare da me dell'oro affinato con il fuoco"); silver = wisdom (purified "seven times," Psalm 12); bronze, tin, copper, iron, and lead = the baser moral states. The smelting imagery of Ezekiel, Isaiah, Malachi, and Jeremiah is mobilised to describe moral purification, with Pentecost fire as the eschatological furnace that fuses the community into "un cuore solo e un'anima sola" (Acts 4:32). The Hebrew urim ve-tummim (אוּרִים וְתֻמִּים), borne by the priests, is explained as "illuminazioni della verità e perfezioni di ogni genere" — a more satisfactory rendering than the Vulgate's "doctrina et veritas."

> *"Sedebit conflans, et emundans argentum, et purgabit filios Levi, qui praecipue purgati esse debent in auro charitatis, et argento sapientiae."*
<cite>p. 615 (Malachi 3:3, cited within Caput IX)</cite>

---

## Caput X: "A che cosa corrispondono alcuni minerali con mirabile artificio"

Mixed minerals — marcasite and alum — occupy the position between stones and metals, corresponding in the body to the intestines (which lie between bones and flesh). In the interior man, these correspond to the natural faculties: intellect, will, the irascible and concupiscible powers. Zorzi reads the plunder of the Temple by Antiochus (1 Maccabees) as an allegory of the devil's spoliation of these powers: the golden altar = will (seat of charity); the candelabrum = intellect; the golden incense-vessels = concupiscible power; the mortars = irascible power. The episode of Belshazzar's feast (Daniel 5) is adduced as a type of the abuse of these sacred vessels.

---

## Caput XI: "Insieme a quali parti del corpo le piante vegetano e verdeggiano"

Vegetative life in the body is found in flesh, blood, and vital spirits. Aristotle's image of man as an "inverted tree" (roots = hair, nourishment taken from above through the mouth) is set alongside the Psalmic "trees of Adonay" (cedars of Lebanon) as figures of celestial plants. The planting of paradise (Genesis 2:8) is etymologised: the Hebrew מקדם (michedem, "from the east" or "from the beginning") is read so that chedem suggests the Father as root and principle of all trees. The chapter ends with the Johannine prayer (John 17:21, "Rogo Pater, ut sicut ego et tu unum sumus") as the telos of the plant-image: the human tree, rooted in the heavenly planter, returns to unity with its source.

> *"Plantat enim has arbores ille, de quo dicitur: Omnis plantatio, quam non plantavit Pater meus coelestis, eradicabitur."*
<cite>p. 622 (Caput XI)</cite>

---

## Caput XII–XIII: Animal Life and Metempsychosis

Chapter XII establishes that animal faculties — sensation, movement, instinct — are present in man, with each species of animal corresponding to a faculty or bodily member. Chapter XIII addresses metempsychosis at length, reviewing the positions of the major ancient schools: Xenocrates, Arcesilaus, Carneades, Ammonius on how to read Plato's transmigration myths; Iamblichus and Porphyry who reject literal transmigration of rational souls into beasts; Proclus, Hermias, and Syrianus who find it monstrous. Zorzi's conclusion follows Porphyry: a morally depraved man is not an ass but "asinino" — having the quality of an ass. This sets up the positive doctrine: what the ancients expressed mythologically through metamorphosis, the sacred authors express as the spiritual transformation of the interior man.

---

## Capp. XIV–XVI: Noah's Ark as Cosmic Image

These chapters interpret Noah's ark (Genesis 6–7) as a comprehensive image of man containing all species within himself. The prescription of seven pairs of clean animals and one pair of unclean (Genesis 7:2) receives a numerological treatment: seven corresponds to the superior goods, one to the material.

---

## Capp. XVII–XXIX: Celestial Correspondences

The longest section maps the heavens onto man. Chapter XVII argues that all the heavens are contained in the human being. Chapters XVIII–XIX treat the zodiacal signs and their distribution over twelve members of the body (duodenary number). Chapter XX–XXIX constitute a planet-by-planet analysis: each planet corresponds to specific faculties, qualities, and bodily regions — Saturn to condensation and fixity, Jupiter to balance and governance, Mars to heat and irascibility, the Sun to vivification and illumination, Venus to softening and love, Mercury to acuity and penetration, the Moon to fluidity and change. Chapter XXX draws the conclusion that the proportional motions of the planets among themselves and relative to the eighth sphere mirror the proportions of our interior forces.

---

## Capp. XXXI–XXXIV: Angels, God, and the Divine Members

The final movement of the section treats the highest correspondences. Chapter XXXI addresses the harmony of disordered things and spiritual "scoria." Chapter XXXII argues that man contains the angels within himself and harmonises with them. Chapter XXXIII treats man as "temple and image of God" singing in concert with God. Chapter XXXIV — "De consonantia membrorum Dei et hominis" — is the climax: Zorzi surveys the scriptural "members" attributed to God (eye, hand, mouth, arm, etc.) as analogical figures of the divine nature, correlating them with human faculties. The Trinitarian structure is mapped via Hermes Trismegistus ("Il Padre di tutte le cose essendo intelletto, vita e splendore, generò l'uomo simile a sé") onto man's intellect, life, and love. The garden of Eden with its Tree of Life (= the Father as source of all rational creatures) and Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil (= Christ, the only being simultaneously *beatus* and *viator*) provides the final Kabbalistic-arboreal image.

> *"Homo est quoddam omne, et quoddam totum in omni, id est in Deo, qui omnia virtute constat, non quidem compositione, homo autem compositione constans est ex omnibus rerum potestatibus."*
<cite>p. 603 (Hermes Trismegistus, cited in Caput V)</cite>

---

## Zorzi's Method and Sources

Throughout, Zorzi's method is the harmonisation of multiple traditions: Kabbalistic letter-hermeneutics (the name Adam, urim ve-tummim), Neoplatonic hierarchies (Proclus on superpartient proportions, Porphyry and Iamblichus on the soul), Hermetic anthropology (Trismegistus on man as "totum in omni"), Aristotelian natural philosophy, Galenic medicine, and scriptural typology. The figures of the Tabernacle and Temple, Noah's Ark, the metals offered for the Temple's construction, and the garden of Eden all function as divinely encoded diagrams of the microcosm. The section as a whole is the most extended and systematic anthropological statement in the *De Harmonia Mundi*, and constitutes Zorzi's fullest elaboration of the claim that "harmonia" is not merely a cosmological property but the very constitution of the human person.
